title: Oily, Greasy Scalp: Balancing the Roots
excerpt: An oily, greasy scalp is one of the most common hair complaints, yet Ayurveda has understood its root cause for centuries: an imbalance of Kapha and Pitta doshas at the scalp. Here is how the classical texts explain it, and what you can do about it every day.
body_html:
why your scalp feels greasy by noon

You wash your hair in the morning and by midday the roots already look flat and slick. You press a tissue near the crown and it comes away translucent. If this sounds familiar, you are not alone, and you are not doing anything wrong. The tendency toward a greasy scalp is often constitutional, and Ayurveda recognised it long ago as a condition linked to two of the three doshas working in excess: Kapha and Pitta.

The classical texts describe the scalp as a site of Sneha, the quality of unctuousness or oiliness. Sneha is not inherently bad; in the right amount it keeps the scalp nourished and the hair lustrous. Problems begin when Kapha, which governs heaviness, moisture, and cohesion in the body, accumulates beyond its normal measure at the head. Pitta, which governs heat and transformation, then works on that excess Kapha and liquefies the natural oils, producing the sticky, greasy feeling (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana).

Bhavaprakasha, the sixteenth-century compendium, describes the qualities of Kapha-dominant conditions of the skin and scalp in similar terms: heaviness, excess moisture, stickiness, and a tendency for impurities to adhere (Bhavaprakasha, Purva Khanda). When you read that description, the greasy scalp makes intuitive sense: it is Kapha in excess, sometimes with Pitta adding heat and inflammation at the root.

what drives kapha and pitta upward

Certain foods and habits reliably push Kapha and Pitta higher. A diet heavy in dairy, sweets, fried food, and sour or fermented items is a consistent trigger. Irregular sleep, skipping meals, and long hours under stress keep Pitta elevated, which in turn unsettles the scalp's oil balance. Sushruta Samhita notes that excess intake of heavy, oily, and sweet foods promotes the buildup of Kapha in the body, and the scalp, being a seat of vital channels linked to the head, reflects this imbalance early (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana).

Washing frequency matters too, but not in the way most people assume. Overwashing with harsh shampoos strips the scalp, which then compensates by producing even more oil. Underwashing allows sebum and dead cells to accumulate, trapping heat and feeding a cycle of greasiness. The classical view would say neither extreme serves the balance of Sneha: the goal is to clean gently and consistently, not aggressively.

Seasonal shifts play a role as well. Late winter and spring are Kapha seasons in Ayurveda, when oiliness, congestion, and heaviness tend to increase throughout the body. If your scalp gets noticeably worse between February and May, this seasonal Kapha rise is a likely contributor.

everyday habits that help

The most practical interventions are dietary and simple. Reducing dairy, cutting back on sweets and fried snacks, and eating more bitter, astringent, and light foods helps bring Kapha down over time. Bitter greens, lentils, ginger in warm water in the morning, and cooked vegetables are all easy to work into Indian home cooking without any special ingredients.

For the scalp directly, rinsing with a dilute decoction of neem leaves or triphala powder in warm water after shampooing has a gentle astringent effect that helps manage excess oil without stripping the scalp. Both neem and triphala appear in the classical texts as remedies for skin and scalp conditions involving Kapha excess (Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu, Haritakyadi Varga). Washing two to three times a week with a mild cleanser is usually more effective than daily washing with a strong one.

Scalp massage, or Shiro Abhyanga, sounds counterintuitive when the scalp is already oily, but a brief dry massage with the fingertips before washing helps loosen sebum and dead skin, improves circulation at the root, and calms excess Pitta-driven inflammation. Keep it short, five to ten minutes, and do it just before you wash, not as an overnight treatment.

Cumin and coriander, both common in everyday cooking, are described in the classical texts as having properties that reduce Kapha and support digestion (Bhavaprakasha, Nighantu). Using them liberally in food is a small but consistent lever for managing the internal Kapha load that eventually shows up at the scalp.

when to see a doctor or vaidya

Most oily scalp conditions respond gradually to dietary and lifestyle adjustments over several weeks. However, please consult a qualified vaidya or a dermatologist if you notice: significant hair loss or thinning along with the oiliness; visible flaking that is yellow, crusty, or accompanied by redness, which may indicate seborrhoeic dermatitis rather than a simple Kapha imbalance; itching that is severe or does not settle with gentle home care; or any sudden change in your scalp that seems linked to a new medication or a hormonal shift.

This article is general information about the Ayurvedic perspective on oily scalp conditions. It is not a diagnosis and is not a substitute for personalised medical or Ayurvedic care. A trained vaidya can assess your specific constitution and give guidance tailored to you.

Try This Today

  • Morning ginger water: Boil a few thin slices of fresh ginger in two cups of water for five minutes. Drink warm first thing in the morning. This gently kindles digestion and helps reduce the internal Kapha load that feeds scalp oiliness.
  • Triphala scalp rinse: Mix one teaspoon of triphala powder in two cups of warm water and stir well. After your regular shampoo, pour this over your scalp, massage briefly, and rinse off. Use two to three times a week for a gentle astringent effect.
  • Pre-wash fingertip massage: Five minutes before washing, use your fingertips (not nails) to massage the scalp in small circular movements. This loosens accumulated sebum and improves circulation at the roots, making your wash more effective.
  • Cut back on dairy for two weeks: Dairy is one of the strongest Kapha-increasing foods. Try removing milk, paneer, and curd from your diet for two weeks and observe whether your scalp feels less oily. If yes, reintroduce small amounts slowly.
  • Wash two or three times a week: Rather than washing daily with a strong shampoo, settle into a rhythm of two to three gentle washes a week. This prevents the rebound oiliness that comes from stripping the scalp's natural oils too often.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Ginger (अदरक)

Reduces Kapha internally; taken as warm water infusion in the morning to lighten the digestive and constitutional Kapha load.

Cumin (जीरा)

Described in Bhavaprakasha as a Kapha-pacifying spice; use freely in daily cooking to support the internal balance that affects scalp oiliness.

Coriander (धनिया)

A cooling, Kapha-reducing herb in classical texts; supports digestion and gently manages the Pitta component of scalp inflammation.

metafields.dek: An oily, greasy scalp is one of the most common hair complaints, yet Ayurveda has understood its root cause for centuries: an imbalance of Kapha and Pitta doshas at the scalp. Here is how the classical texts explain it, and what you can do about it every day.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: An oily scalp in Ayurveda is a Kapha-Pitta imbalance, not just poor hygiene.
Dietary changes, especially cutting dairy and sweets, are the most reliable long-term fix.
Gentle herbal rinses and moderate washing frequency work better than harsh, frequent shampooing.

glossary_terms.definition: One of the three doshas, governing heaviness, moisture, and cohesion in the body; when excess, it produces oiliness and stagnation.
The dosha of heat and transformation; excess Pitta at the scalp increases inflammation and liquefies oils, worsening greasiness.
Unctuousness or oiliness; a quality that is nourishing in balance but problematic in excess, especially at the scalp.
Head massage; in the context of an oily scalp, a brief dry massage before washing helps loosen sebum and calm Pitta.
A classical Ayurvedic formulation of three fruits (amalaki, bibhitaki, haritaki) with gentle astringent and cleansing properties.

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Kapha" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Kapha is defined as: One of the three doshas, governing heaviness, moisture, and cohesion in the body; when excess, it produces oiliness and stagnation.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to morning ginger water. Boil a few thin slices of fresh ginger in two cups of water for five minutes. Drink warm first thing in the morning. This gently kindles digestion and helps reduce the internal Kapha load that feeds scalp oiliness.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Ginger for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Ginger is highly regarded. Reduces Kapha internally; taken as warm water infusion in the morning to lighten the digestive and constitutional Kapha load.
